summary: I was born in Mogadishu, Somalia in 1988. It is where my life began before the hardships that followed. Although I left Mogadishu at a young age, it remains an important part of my identity and memories.

Somalia is my homeland, where I was born and where my culture comes from. Even though I left Somalia as a child, I still hold on to Somali traditions and cultural clothes. I hope to return someday to bring back the things I lost and keep our culture alive here in Minnesota.

After leaving Somalia, I grew up in a refugee camp in Ethiopia. It was a difficult time, but it was where I spent my childhood before resettlement. Ethiopia was a place of waiting and hope for a new life elsewhere.

I arrived in the United States on December 7, 2011, after being resettled. The U.S. has given me opportunities for work, education, and a better future. Though I faced challenges like losing my suitcase and adjusting to new places, I am grateful for the support from the government and the chance to build a new life here.

My first stop in the United States was New York City, where I landed and experienced some confusion and challenges. I lost one of my suitcases there, which had precious photos and cultural items. I stayed one night in a New York hotel before continuing my journey to Texas.

After New York, I flew to Amarillo, Texas, where I lived for about nine months. I worked in a meat company in Cactus, Texas, which was hard and cold work. Despite the difficulties, I was relieved when I got my first paycheck, which helped me support my children and basic needs.

I decided to move to Minnesota because life seemed better there than in Texas. Now I have work and school opportunities, thanks to the government’s support. Minnesota is where I am building my future, continuing my education and work to improve my life and keep my culture alive.
